# Code review — indexit Telegram PoC (spec-compliance)

Scope: the implementation under `main.go` + `internal/**`, reviewed **against the
specification** in `.github/notes/Telegram fetcher, PoC implementation plan.md`.
This pass only records places where the code diverges from what the plan
*fixes as a contract* — not general Go-quality issues.

Deliberately **not** duplicated here (already filed elsewhere):

- implementation-quality findings — see `reviews/20260517T103045Z.md`
  (mutex copy, pagination guards, logout consistency, append default,
  `slog.Default` in fetch/rate, RateGuard-retries-once, mapper/golden test
  gaps, etc.);
- plan gaps already audited in `reports/20260517T101916Z.md`
  (`INDEXIT_LOG_LEVEL` unimplemented; `-v=debug` instead of `-v=info`;
  no transport backoff; summary missing `flood_waits`; GoReleaser `tool`
  identity; partial test strategy) and `reports/20260517T101938Z.md`
  (`raw_hash` missing; `connecting` has no DC; FLOOD_WAIT wording; split
  summary lines; `-vv` no TL names; package layout vs §11; smoke-only golden);
- release/CI findings — see `reviews/20260517T103458Z.md`.

The findings below are the spec discrepancies those four documents did **not**
cover. Build state: `go test ./...` green; `go vet ./...` still flags the
known mutex copy (`internal/log/log.go:117,126`, see prior review §2.1).

Severity legend: **Critical** (data loss / wrong behaviour / panic potential),
**High** (likely bug or footgun), **Medium** (contract not honoured, will bite
later), **Low** (minor), **Nit** (style).

## 1. Observability contract (§10)

### 1.1 `PEER_FLOOD` is returned bare — the "fail fast **with guidance**" half of §8 is missing — Severity: **Medium**

`internal/telegram/rate.go:32-34`:

```go
if tgerr.Is(err, "PEER_FLOOD") {
    return err
}
```

Plan §8 specifies RateGuard as the *central handler* for
`PEER_FLOOD (fail fast with guidance)`. The code gets the "fail fast" right
(no retry, no sleep — `rate_test.go:78-83` even pins that), but there is **no
guidance**: the user sees the raw `rpc error code 420: PEER_FLOOD` with no hint
about what it means or what to do (the account is sending requests too
aggressively; back off, reduce `--limit`, retry later). FLOOD_WAIT gets a
human line (`rate.go:61`) and cold-cache gets `ColdPeerHint`; `PEER_FLOOD` is
the one error class the plan calls out for guidance and the one that ships
without it.

**Fix.** Wrap with an actionable sentence the way `ColdPeerHint` does, e.g.
`fmt.Errorf("PEER_FLOOD: Telegram is rate-limiting this account; reduce request volume or wait before retrying: %w", err)`.

### 1.2 Always-on liveness markers are emitted at `Info`, so the §10.1 tiering cannot be realised — Severity: **Medium**

§10.1 fixes a two-tier model: at the **default level (`warn`)** a fixed set of
lifecycle markers is *always on* (`connecting…`, `authorized…`, `proxy:…`,
`FLOOD_WAIT…`, `cache: loaded/persisted`, the summary, and the heartbeat —
"this guarantees the user sees *something* … even at the default verbosity");
**per-page progress** (`dialogs: page …`) appears only at **`-v` (`info`)**.

In the implementation there is no `warn`-vs-`info` separation — *both* layers
are logged at `Info`:

- lifecycle markers: `internal/telegram/api.go:87,102,111` (`connecting` /
  `still connecting` / `connected`), `auth.go:21` (`authorized`),
  `command/telegram/common.go:67` + `auth.go:75` (`proxy`),
  `rate.go:61` (`flood wait`), `api.go:148` (heartbeat), and the
  `cache: loaded` / `cache: persisted` / `done` lines in
  `command/telegram/fetch.go:94,110,112,158,183,185`;
- per-page progress: `internal/telegram/fetch.go:97,110,216,221` — also `Info`.

This only *appears* to work because the level mapping is itself off-spec:
`internal/log/log.go:44-51` sets the **default level to `info`** (already filed
as a discrepancy in `reports/20260517T101916Z.md` §2). The two off-spec choices
cancel out. The consequence not yet recorded: **correcting the default level to
`warn` (as §3/§10.1 require) would silently suppress every mandated always-on
marker**, because `Info < Warn`. §10.1 explicitly says removing a
previously-printed lifecycle marker "counts as a UX regression" — yet the
straightforward fix to the level mapping causes exactly that.

**Fix.** Give the always-on markers a level that survives the default
(emit them at `Warn`, or add a dedicated "lifecycle" path that ignores the
verbosity gate), and keep per-page progress at `Info`. Then the default can be
set to `warn` per spec without losing liveness.

### 1.3 Lifecycle-marker wording diverges for `authorized` and `proxy` (and `authorized` bypasses the injected logger) — Severity: **Low**

`reports/20260517T101938Z.md` §2-3 already filed the `connecting` and
`FLOOD_WAIT` wording mismatches. Two more markers from the §10.1 list differ:

| §10.1 contract                       | Implementation                                            |
|--------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------|
| `authorized as @<username> (id=<n>)` | `authorized user_id=<n> username=<u> name=<…>` (`auth.go:21`) |
| `proxy: <type> <host>:<port>`        | `proxy type=<t> host=<h> port=<p>` (`common.go:67`)       |

Wording "may evolve" per §10.1, so this is Low — but it is part of the fixed
example set, and the README (`README.md:75-76`) further entrenches the
divergent forms.

Separately, `Login` emits its marker through `slog.Default()`
(`internal/telegram/auth.go:21`) rather than the logger threaded through the
context — the same class flagged for fetch/rate in
`reviews/20260517T103045Z.md` §3.1, but `auth.go` is a third site not listed
there. At `auth login` the "authorized" marker therefore only surfaces because
`log.Setup` mutates the global default.

## 2. CLI surface (§3)

### 2.1 `--timeout` diverges from the §3 surface in type and placement — Severity: **Low**

§3 lists `--timeout` as a **`fetch` common flag** of type **`duration`**
(default `0`). The implementation declares it on the **`telegram` command** as a
**`string`**:

`internal/command/telegram/root.go:10,25`:

```go
timeout string
...
command.PersistentFlags().StringVar(&opt.timeout, "timeout", "0", "wall-clock timeout for Telegram operations")
```

then parses it by hand (`common.go:32`, `time.ParseDuration(opt.timeout)`).
Three consequences:

1. `--help` shows `string` with default `"0"` instead of a duration flag.
2. A bare number is rejected — `--timeout=30` fails with
   `time: missing unit in duration "30"` (exit via `fmt.Errorf`, not even a
   usage code), whereas a real `DurationVar` would still reject it but a user
   reading the `duration` type would at least know to write `30s`.
3. Because it is persistent on `telegram`, it is also exposed on
   `auth login/status/logout` — outside the §3 scope, which scopes it to
   `fetch`.

It is also absent from the README root-flags table (`README.md:81-85`).

**Fix.** Use `DurationVar` and either move it under `fetch` or document the
broadened scope.

### 2.2 `debug uid` prints the raw `PeerRef` struct, not the canonical UID — Severity: **Low**

Milestone 1's demoable is `indexit telegram debug uid <s>` *echoes canonical*.
`internal/command/telegram/debug.go:34-36` JSON-encodes the parsed `PeerRef`
with no field tags, so it emits Go field names and internal bookkeeping
(`{"Kind":"channel","ID":123,"Username":"","TopicID":45,"HasTopic":true,
"AnchorID":0,"HasAnchor":false}`) instead of the canonical string the type
already knows how to produce — `PeerRef.String()` (`uid.go:73`) yields
`channel:123:45`. The "canonical" round-trip the milestone promises is one call
away but unused.

**Fix.** Emit `ref.String()` (optionally alongside the struct under a verbose
flag), or add `json:"..."` tags so the echo is at least snake-cased and
schema-consistent with the rest of the output.

## 3. Output & documentation contract (§6, milestone 9)

### 3.1 The JSONL record schema and its additive-compatibility contract are undocumented — Severity: **Low**

§6.2 fixes the compatibility contract as documentation: *"Versioned
`_schema_version` is not included in PoC; the discriminator + field-additive
policy is documented as the compatibility contract."* Milestone 9's demoable is
"Output format documented." The README documents *that* output is JSONL on
stdout (`README.md:105-133`) but never lists the `_kind` discriminator, the
dialog/message field sets (§6.1/§6.2), or the "fields may be added, never
repurposed" promise. A downstream Sparkle indexer — the stated consumer (§1
success criteria) — has no written schema to code against and no statement of
the stability guarantee.

**Fix.** Add a short "Output schema" section enumerating the two record kinds
and the additive policy (this also pairs naturally with the golden-byte test
gap already filed in the prior report §6.1 / §7).

## 4. Repository baseline (§2 / milestone 0)

### 4.1 `viper` is linked into the binary despite §5.1's "no viper dependency" — Severity: **Low**

§5.1: *"The implementation lives in a small `internal/config` package
(godotenv-style parser, no viper dependency)."* The package itself is indeed
viper-free (`internal/config/parser.go`), but `viper` and its transitive
encoders are still compiled into the binary:

```
$ go list -deps ./... | grep -i viper
github.com/spf13/viper
github.com/spf13/viper/internal/encoding/{hcl,json,toml,yaml}
github.com/mitchellh/mapstructure
github.com/subosito/gotenv
```

They arrive through the inherited template's `go.octolab.org/toolkit/*`
dependencies (`go.mod:11-12,49-51`). The intent of §5.1 — keep the config path
viper-free — is met at the package boundary but defeated at the link boundary,
and milestone 0 ("repo/toolchain refresh") is therefore not fully done. Same
root cause as the still-`tool` GoReleaser identity already filed in
`reports/20260517T101916Z.md` §5.

**Fix.** Trim the template dependencies that pull viper, or accept it and drop
the "no viper dependency" wording from the plan so the contract matches reality.

## 5. Nits

### 5.1 `auth status` logs the `proxy:` line twice when a session exists — Nit

`internal/command/telegram/auth.go:72-79` logs the proxy descriptor, then
(when the session file exists) calls `newClient`, which logs the identical line
again at `common.go:67-71`. With no session the line prints once; with a session
it prints twice. Harmless, but noisy for a diagnostic command.

## Suggested triage order

1. §1.2 (marker leveling) — blocks correctly fixing the default log level
   (`reports/...101916Z` §2); fix them together or the level fix is a UX
   regression.
2. §1.1 (`PEER_FLOOD` guidance) — small, completes the §8 error-handling
   contract.
3. §2.1 (`--timeout` type/placement) — visible in `--help`; cheap.
4. §3.1 (schema docs) — pair with the golden-byte test work already queued.
5. §2.2, §1.3, §4.1, §5.1 — opportunistic.
